findings.message: `identifiers.rs` is 854 lines long, which exceeds the repository's 700-line hard limit for a module. The extra size is mostly from co-locating the large test block with the implementation, so this already violates the stated module-size rule and makes future changes harder to keep coherent.

`ModulePath::from_identifiers` is a new public constructor, but there is no unit test covering its happy path. That leaves a public API path in this module without the required test coverage, so a regression in this constructor would not be caught by the current suite.

`ItemAction` derives `strum::Display`/`EnumString` without a rename policy, so it accepts/emits `Add`, `Modify`, etc. The inherited TDDD-03 catalogue contract uses lowercase tags (`add`, `modify`, `reference`, `delete`), and the existing codec/fixtures use those values. Unless this enum is renamed with `#[strum(serialize_all = "snake_case")]` (and covered by tests), valid catalogue actions will be rejected or re-emitted with the wrong spelling once the v2 codec maps through these domain strings.

`SelfReceiver` currently parses/displays the Rust variant names (`Owned`, `SharedRef`, `ExclusiveRef`), but ADR D8 defines the catalogue values as the receiver tokens `self`, `&self`, and `&mut self`. This makes the domain `FromStr` reject the receiver strings already used in catalogue data and will force every caller to duplicate a custom mapping instead of using the validated domain enum. Add explicit strum serializations for the three variants and tests for the token forms.

`Layer` also derives default strum casing, so `Layer::Domain.to_string()` is `Domain` and parsing `domain` fails. ADR D1 defines the layer axis as the lowercase catalogue values `domain` / `usecase` / `infrastructure`; with the current implementation, valid v2 documents using the specified layer values cannot round-trip through the domain enum. Add the appropriate strum serialization policy and tests for the lowercase spellings.

The Phase 2 classification for `S Ōł® C` conflicts with the skip rule below it: the table marks structural matches as `­¤öĄ`, but the later `skip` rule says `action=Reference` + structural equality should be suppressed instead of reported. As written, the same case is both a blue signal and a skip.

`D3` says this is a `10 ķĀśÕ¤¤` table, but the table itself enumerates 11 distinct conditions, and the consequence note repeats the same `10 ķĀśÕ¤¤` claim. The count is internally inconsistent; either the heading/bullets need to say 11 or one row needs to be merged.

`transition_methods` is allowed to reference `trait_impls`ÕåģŃü« `MethodDeclaration` here, but D10 later removes `methods` from `TraitImplDecl` entirely. That leaves no schema location for the referenced method declarations, so the typestate transition path is internally inconsistent as written.

The Phase 1 `Delete` step removes an item from `S` but never rewrites or rejects surviving references to that item. For any deletion of a still-referenced baseline item, `S` will end up with dangling `Id`s, so the stated fully-resolved `S` cannot actually be constructed.

ADR 1 says the signal evaluator refines action semantics into a "10-region" table, but the referenced ADR 3 D3 defines an 11-region table and repeats that count later. Because ADR 1 explicitly points readers to ADR 3 D3 as the canonical region definition, this cross-reference leaves implementers with conflicting signal-table cardinality.

This assigns implicit Reference resolution for undeclared baseline-derived items to the codec, but ADR 2 D10 keeps the A codec open-world and defers resolution to S construction, while ADR 3 D4 says the signal evaluator dynamically attaches Reference to B. That is a component-boundary contradiction and can lead to implementing baseline-dependent behavior in the catalogue codec.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items declared for deletion, but the pseudocode adds the A/catalogue element to D (`D.add(elt rebuild_id)`) rather than the removed baseline item from S/B. Following this literally makes D contain the delete declaration shape instead of the baseline item, which contradicts the D definition and can corrupt delete progress evaluation and D-specific external_crates rebuilding.

The signal distribution summary omits `S \ C + action=Add`, even though the table immediately above classifies that region as Yellow. This makes the summary inconsistent with the canonical table and risks dropping the new-declare-not-yet-implemented case from implementations that follow the summary.

ADR 3 defines the Phase 2 table as 11 regions, but ADR 1 still references a '10-region' signal table. That leaves the catalogue ADR pointing at the wrong evaluator contract for action semantics; update the ADR 1 D4/Related text to match ADR 3's 11-region table, or adjust ADR 3 if 10 was intended.

The canonical schema prose introduces `ItemAction`, and ADR 2/3 consume `ItemAction`, but the ADR 1 schema snippets declare `action: TypeAction` for TypeEntry/TraitEntry/FunctionEntry. This is a semantic type-name mismatch in the plan artifacts and will send implementers toward a non-selected action type.

This states `S.external_crates` is the full union of B and A, but the same paragraph says deletes can remove dependencies from S and leave them only in D. A full BŌł¬A union cannot drop a baseline-only dependency used solely by a deleted item, so the S/D construction contract is internally inconsistent and may make Phase 2 compare against the wrong target graph.

D is defined as the set of B-derived items being deleted, but the Delete branch iterates over A and adds `elt` to D. That contradicts the definition and can lose the baseline item's actual structure/references; the branch should make clear that D receives the matched baseline/S item removed by the delete operation, not the catalogue delete declaration itself.

ADR 2 cites its own D6 as the decision establishing '1 catalogue = 1 crate', but D6 is about fixed values for rustdoc fields; the crate-per-catalogue decision is ADR 1 D6. This broken decision reference appears in the codec rationale and rejected alternative text, so readers are directed to the wrong design decision.

D1 says the Layer axis is determined implicitly from catalogue file placement, but D6 defines an explicit `layer: Layer` field inside `CatalogueDocument` and says the document declares its layer. This leaves the schema contract ambiguous: implementers cannot tell whether layer is derived from path/layout or parsed from the JSON field.

D1 states that A/S extend `rustdoc_types::Crate` with action only and that role/pattern/docs remain on the Catalogue side, but D7 later says catalogue `docs` are encoded into rustdoc `Item.docs`. Those two rules conflict over whether docs are part of the TypeGraph produced by the codec.

The Related section still describes the signal evaluator ADR as a future planned ADR, while this ADR already references the concrete ADR 3 decisions earlier and the scoped change includes that ADR. This makes the cross-document relationship stale and self-contradictory; it should point to `2026-05-08-0305-tddd-signal-evaluator-three-way-diff.md` as the sibling/follow-up ADR already present.

ADR 1 D11 explicitly uses `FunctionPath` because function short names such as `new`/`build` commonly collide, but ADR 2 makes graph identity `name`-only. That means two allowed catalogue functions with the same short name in different modules collapse during matching/Id rebuild, so the plan cannot correctly evaluate a catalogue shape that ADR 1 says is valid.

The external-crate construction rule contradicts itself: this passage says `S.external_crates` is rebuilt only from items remaining after Delete, so delete-only dependencies disappear from S, but the same ADR's pseudocode and Reassess section describe `S.external_crates = B Ōł¬ A`, and ADR 2 D5 says to union B and A external crates. Those two rules produce different S graphs whenever a deleted baseline item was the only user of an external crate.

ADR 1 says undeclared baseline-derived entries are auto-resolved as implicit References by the codec, but ADR 2/3 place that responsibility in the signal evaluator: A codec is open-world and independent of B, while Phase 1 builds S from B with dynamic `Reference` attachment. Leaving this as-is gives conflicting ownership for whether the codec may read/resolve baseline items.

ADR 2 now defines identity as short name for types/traits but full FunctionPath for functions, while ADR 3 Phase 1 still says all identity matching and Id rebuilds are by name. That makes functions like different `new`/`build` entries collide or be matched incorrectly in S/D/C construction, contradicting ADR 1 D11 and ADR 2 D3.

The signal table classifies `S \ C + action=Add` as Yellow, but the immediately following Yellow distribution omits that region. If implementers use the distribution summary, declared-but-unimplemented adds can be dropped from Yellow reporting despite being part of the canonical table.

D1/D7 state that catalogue docs are encoded into `rustdoc Item.docs`, but D2 says `role / pattern / docs` remain only on `CatalogueDocument`. This leaves the TypeGraph/codec contract ambiguous about whether docs are present in A/S at all, despite render/documentation being listed as a consumer.

D11 says unprefixed TypeRef identifiers always mean same-catalogue declarations, but the ADR examples rely on unprefixed standard/prelude types such as `Result`, `Option`, `Vec`, and `String`. With the stated open-world/closed-world flow, those become unresolved markers and then Phase 1 errors unless the catalogue/baseline declares them, so common declared TypeRefs are physically unexecutable without an additional std/prelude resolution rule or prefix requirement.

`bool` is listed with `Vec`/`Option`/`String` as a prefix-less std item that the codec should resolve as a `"std"` external-crate reference. That contradicts the `rustdoc_types` mapping described in D9, where Rust primitives should become `Type::Primitive`; encoding `bool` as a resolved external path will make catalogue-derived A structurally differ from rustdoc-derived B/C for valid fields such as `active: bool`. Split primitive handling from std-prelude path handling.

D2 states immediately above that functions are identified by `FunctionPath`, but the action branches still do `assert elt in S (by name)` / `get_by_name`. If implemented literally, duplicate function names in different modules, which ADR 1 D11 explicitly supports via `FunctionPath`, become ambiguous during Add/Modify/Reference/Delete processing. The pseudocode needs to use the same identity key as the surrounding text: short name for types/traits, full FunctionPath for functions.

ADR 3 narrows function identity to `FunctionPath (module_path + name)`, but ADR 2 D3 defines `FunctionPath = crate_name + module_path + name` and says all segments are used. If the signal evaluator follows ADR 3, it can build identity keys that do not match the catalogue key shape from ADR 1/2, and same-module/name functions from different crates would collapse whenever graphs are aggregated. Keep the crate_name segment in the ADR 3 identity rule or explicitly state why this evaluator is strictly single-crate and how the full catalogue key is normalized.

The Delete branch rejects as soon as the deleted item is still referenced by any current S item, but the same Phase 1 loop also allows Modify to replace items. A valid change that deletes `OldType` and modifies `User` to remove its `OldType` field can be rejected if Delete is processed before Modify, making the algorithm order-dependent and contradicting the stated goal that S is the final result of applying A to B. The plan needs to apply Add/Modify replacements before dangling-reference validation, or run that validation after final S construction.

D11 now says prefixless prelude types like `Vec`, `Option`, `Result`, `String`, and `Box` are resolved as a `std` external crate, but D5 still defines `external_crates` extraction as only `TraitImplDecl.origin_crate` plus crate-prefixed `TypeRef`s. A catalogue using `Vec<UserId>` would therefore create a `ResolvedPath` to `std` without the corresponding `external_crates` entry/crate_id, making the codec rules internally inconsistent.

Phase 1 checks for dangling IDs before resolving A's unresolved TypeRef markers, then resolves those markers against raw B. If a catalogue Add/Modify references a baseline item that is also Delete-declared, the pre-resolution dangling check cannot see that reference, and `B.find_by_name` can still resolve it after the item has been moved to D. That either leaves S referencing a deleted item or reintroduces it, contradicting the stated requirement that references to deleted items be rejected as a Phase 1 Error. The marker resolution needs to run before the dangling-reference validation, or resolve only against the post-delete S universe.

ADR 3 says DeleteÕć”ńÉåÕŠīŃü« S is the closed-world universe used to reject references to deleted types, but the same paragraph also resolves against `A.index`. Since A still contains catalogue items including Delete declarations, a reference to a deleted item can resolve through A after it was removed from S, accepting the exact case this section says must become a Phase 1 Error. Remove the A fallback or restrict it to non-delete items already retained in S.

ADR 2 still defines unresolved marker resolution as `B (Baseline) + A` and says the universe is `B + A`, while ADR 3 now defines the closed-world universe as post-delete S. Following ADR 2 would allow deleted baseline items from B to resolve unresolved TypeRefs, contradicting ADR 3's delete-after-S invariant and defeating deleted-reference rejection. Update D10 and the matching consequence text to use the post-delete S rule.

Phase 1.5 says unresolved markers must resolve only against the post-Delete S universe so references to deleted types are rejected, but the pseudocode still falls back to `A.find_by_name(...)`. Because `A` still contains Delete declarations, this branch can resolve a marker to an item that was intentionally removed from S, contradicting the stated closed-world rule and allowing the exact deleted-type reference the plan says must fail.

This consequence still says Phase 1 rejects markers that cannot resolve in `B + A`, while D10 now defines the resolver as post-Delete S only. That stale universe definition conflicts with the updated Delete handling and would preserve deleted declarations as valid resolution targets if implemented from this summary.

Rejected alternative H claims ADR 1 D10 adopted optional trait impl methods, but ADR 1 D10 actually removes `TraitImplDecl.methods` entirely and the next sentence says the same. This self-contradiction leaves the rejected-alternative rationale pointing at a decision state that no longer exists.

[TypeRef](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/domain-types.json#L221) is documented as rejecting `::` module-path separators, but T005 requires extracting crate prefixes from `TypeRef` to auto-build `external_crates` ([plan.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/plan.md#L32)) and [spec.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/spec.json#L173) explicitly depends on that prefix-based extraction. As written, the codec cannot represent any external type path.

`SignalRegion` is described as encoding 11 evaluation regions, but the enum lists 12 variants ([domain-types.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/domain-types.json#L1062)). That clashes with the repeated `11 regions` wording in [spec.json](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/spec.json#L193) and [plan.md](/home/flip451/individual/t-rust/templates/SoTOHE-core/track/items/tddd-v2-2026-05-08/plan.md#L42), so T007 does not have a single unambiguous table shape.

The spec says Phase 2 uses a "12 ķĀśÕ¤¤ ├Ś signal table" while citing ADR 3 D3, but the implementation plan for T007 correctly says ADR 3 D3 is an 11-region table. The same 12-region wording is repeated for IN-12 and AC-08, so the plan artifacts disagree on the required evaluator coverage and tests. Either align the spec to the ADR/plan's 11 logical regions, or explicitly distinguish 12 enum variants from 11 logical regions as domain-types.json does.

T004 requires implementing a concrete `BaselineRustdocCodec`, but the infrastructure type catalogue and contract map only define `BaselineRustdocCodecError`; the secondary-adapter list includes `CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodec`, `SignalEvaluatorV2`, and `FsCatalogueLoader`, with no baseline codec node or SOT chain. This leaves a task-required implementation outside the catalogue/contract artifacts, so the plan is not traceable: either add the adapter entry and its contract edges/spec refs, or remove the codec from T004 if raw `rustdoc_types::Crate` deserialization is intended.

IN-12 says Phase 2 covers `S Ōł® C` only for `µ¦ŗķĆĀõĖĆĶć┤ + actionÕłź`, but AC-08 and T007 require handling `S Ōł® C + µ¦ŗķĆĀõĖŹõĖĆĶć┤` as a distinct signal region. This makes the canonical spec internally inconsistent and can lead implementers to omit the mismatch rows from the 3-way table despite the acceptance test requiring them.

T006 is ordered before T007 and is required to implement `SignalEvaluatorPort` returning `Result<ThreeWayEvaluationReport, Phase1Error>`, but T007 is the task that introduces `ThreeWayEvaluationReport` and the related Phase 2 report types. With the stated sequential dependency `T006 Õ«īõ║å ŌåÆ T007 ńØĆµēŗ`, T006 cannot complete the port as described without a type that the plan defers to the next task.

`BaselineRustdocCodec` is introduced as a filesystem-loading `secondary_adapter`, but its catalogue entry has `implements: []` and the contract map shows no port edge for it. That leaves the new Baseline/Current rustdoc JSON dependency outside the trait boundary, unlike the other new adapters, so the plan is not hexagonal/traceable for B/C loading. Add a secondary port for this loader or reclassify it as an infrastructure-internal helper instead of an adapter contract item.

T004 requires implementing a concrete `BaselineRustdocCodec`, but the infrastructure catalogue only declares `BaselineRustdocCodecError` and the contract map has no `BaselineRustdocCodec` adapter node or edge. That leaves a task-required implementation outside the plan artifacts' SOT chain, so the implementation plan is not traceable for the baseline/current rustdoc loader.

T008 is ordered before the catalogue rewrite but still requires `cargo make ci` to be green. By this point T003 has removed the old `TypeCatalogueDocument` read path, while T009 later says the existing `*-types.json` files must still be converted because the new codec cannot read the old schema. That makes the T008 CI gate infeasible; the catalogue rewrite has to happen before the first full CI/verify gate that uses the new codec.

`CatalogueLinter` is kept as an unmodified reference port whose signature still takes `&TypeCatalogueDocument`, but the same catalogue marks `TypeCatalogueDocument` for deletion. Once the old schema is removed, this referenced port cannot remain unchanged, so the plan contradicts itself about whether old catalogue schema consumers are migrated.

The codec error catalogue says crate-prefixed TypeRef values can fail as `UnknownCrateName`, but the same plan requires external_crates to be auto-built from every TraitImplDecl origin crate and TypeRef crate prefix. Under ADR 2 D5/IN-10, a prefix like `domain_core::UserId` is supposed to be collected and assigned a per-graph crate_id, not rejected as unresolved. If implemented as written, valid cross-crate references would be treated as codec errors.

`CatalogueToExtendedCrateCodecError` adds `ExternalCrateUnresolvable`, but the plan/spec says the A codec is open-world: crate-prefixed and std-allowlisted references are auto-collected into `external_crates`, while unresolved catalogue-local references are carried as markers and rejected later by Phase 1. The domain port also returns `NewTypeGraphCodecError`, whose catalogue entry explicitly limits codec errors to parse/ambiguity cases. This extra codec-level rejection path contradicts the declared TypeRef/external_crates semantics and would either be unreachable or force the adapter to violate the port contract.

The usecase catalogue still marks both reference ports as cat-spec yellow, but the briefing says this layer should be 2 blue / 0 yellow / 0 red. That leaves the usecase signal summary out of sync with the artifact.

The domain catalogue now has more than the single accepted yellow: `ContractMapWriter`, `CatalogueLinter`, and `SchemaExporter` are cat-spec yellow in addition to the intended `TypeKindV2` yellow. That contradicts the briefing's 57 blue / 1 yellow / 0 red summary and means the domain yellow budget is exceeded.

This entry says the contract-map renderer update for the new TypeGraph format is out of scope, but the spec includes the required `contract_map_render` rewrite in IN-13/AC-09 and OS-06 only defers detailed TypeKind/Role rendering. If implementers follow this catalogue wording, T008 can skip the renderer migration and fail the planned acceptance criteria.

AC-07 says Phase 1 takes `A + B + C` to build `S` and `D`, but the rest of the plan and ADR 3 split this as Phase 1 = `A + B` construction and Phase 2 = `S / D / C` comparison. This acceptance criterion can drive the wrong tests or implementation by making `C` a Phase 1 dependency when it should only be used in Phase 2.

`ThreeWayEvaluationReport` is described as containing signals for ŌĆ£all evaluated items,ŌĆØ but ADR 3 D3 defines the `S Ōł® C + Reference + match` region as `skip` specifically to reduce output by not emitting those maintained items. As written, the report contract would include skipped reference matches, contradicting the intended signal output semantics.

`CatalogueDocumentError` declares `DuplicateTypeName` / `DuplicateTraitName` / `DuplicateFunctionPath`, but the document is modeled directly as `BTreeMap`s. If the new codec deserializes straight into those maps, duplicate keys are collapsed before validation, so these errors cannot be observed as written unless you add a raw intermediate form or a custom duplicate-preserving parse step.

`InMemoryCatalogueLinter` is declared as a referenced adapter that is ŌĆ£not modified in this track,ŌĆØ but `domain-types.json` changes the `CatalogueLinter` port signature to take `&CatalogueDocument` and explicitly says the adapter's implements edge is preserved. Since an implementation of a modified trait must update its method signature, leaving this adapter as reference-only makes the plan internally inconsistent and can leave T008 with a stale `TypeCatalogueDocument` implementation after the old schema is deleted.

This transition is no longer status-only: `domain-types.json:281-307` adds a new `IdentifierError`, and `domain-types.md:13-14` promotes `CompositePattern`/`VariantPayload` to blue even though `plan.md:13` still leaves T002 as todo. That makes the rendered plan describe a later semantic state than the task graph actually permits.
